title: Comfrey leaf compress for bruises
subtitle: An external wound-supportive compress for soft-tissue injury
excerpt: Dried comfrey leaf steeped as a strong infusion, cooled, and applied as a compress to unbroken bruised skin. External use only. Traditionally used for closed bruises and minor soft-tissue injury.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, comfrey entry: the external wound-supportive tradition, the allantoin action on tissue. the herbal reference on Symphyti radix et herba, the traditional use for bruises, sprains, and muscle soreness as an external application.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The infusion can be made up to 4 hours ahead and stored in the fridge. Dried comfrey leaf stores 12 months in a sealed tin.

glossaryTerms.definition: A compound found in comfrey (and plantain) associated with the promotion of new cell growth and tissue repair. External application is well-tolerated; internal use is not recommended in modern western herbal practice.

content.text: Comfrey (Symphytum officinale, family Boraginaceae) is the traditional wound herb of European herbal medicine. Its

content and soothing gel are the active compounds the tradition draws on for wound support. The herb is used externally for bruises, sprains, and muscle soreness. This preparation is a strong infusion of dried comfrey leaf, cooled and applied as a compress to unbroken bruised skin for 20-30 minutes.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

External use only. This preparation must not be taken internally. Comfrey contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids (PAs) which are harmful to the liver when ingested. Internal use is not accepted in modern western herbal practice. The alkaloid level in the leaf is lower than in the root, but external use only is the correct boundary for this preparation.

Not on broken skin. Do not apply comfrey preparations to open wounds, broken skin, or deep cuts. Comfrey accelerates surface tissue closure so fast that it can trap infection beneath. The skin must be intact.

Not for prolonged use. Use for no more than 10 days continuously on the same site. the European herbal standard guidance states no more than 4-6 weeks per year total use.

Bruises that need medical attention. Any bruise following a significant impact, any bruise with associated pain on movement that might indicate a fracture, bruising around the eye following head injury, or bruising in an unusual pattern should be assessed by a doctor before applying any preparation.

items.prepNote: dried comfrey leaf, not root; confirmed from a reputable supplier

content.text: Place the dried comfrey leaf in a saucepan. Pour over the hot water. Cover and steep for 15 minutes. The infusion will be dark olive-green and smell distinctly herbal and slightly earthy. Strain through a sieve into a bowl. Allow to cool until comfortable on the skin (test on the inside of the wrist first). Soak a clean cloth (cotton or linen), wring out lightly, and apply to the bruise with gentle pressure. Hold in place for 20-30 minutes. Repeat two or three times a day for up to 10 days.

Apply for 20-30 minutes per session, up to three times a day, for up to 10 days on the same site. the European herbal standard guidance: no more than 10 days' continuous use, no more than 4-6 weeks total use per year.
The prepared infusion keeps 24 hours in the fridge in a sealed container. Dried comfrey leaf stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark.
When not to use this remedy
Broken or open skin. On a child under three. Any bruise with possible fracture, head injury, or unusual pattern needs medical assessment.
